title: Today's Tech Brief: The Download: Trump’s new AI order, and smart glasses for warfare
summary: A look at Trump's new AI order and smart glasses for warfare
imageAlt: Image from source article for The Download: Trump’s new AI order, and smart glasses for warfare
imageCaption: The Download: Trump’s new AI order, and smart glasses for warfare
imageSourceTitle: The Download: Trump’s new AI order, and smart glasses for warfare

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and this is your tech brief. Let's dive into today's top stories. First up, President Trump has issued a new executive order on artificial intelligence. The order aims to promote the development and use of AI in various industries, while also addressing concerns around bias and transparency. I am looking at the five key points in this order, and what stands out to me is the focus on making AI more accessible to small businesses and startups. This could be a game-changer for many industries, and I'm curious to see how it plays out.

In other news, a company called Skydio has developed smart glasses designed for use in warfare. These glasses use AI to provide soldiers with real-time information and enhanced situational awareness on the battlefield. My read is that this tech could significantly change the way military operations are conducted, but it's also raising questions about the ethics of using AI in combat. It's a lot to wrap your head around, and I'm not sure I have all the answers.

Another story caught my eye - a bug hunter has leaked Microsoft exploits in defiance of the company's handling of vulnerability disclosures. This is not the first time a researcher has taken this approach, and it highlights the ongoing tension between security researchers and tech companies over how to handle vulnerability disclosures. It's a tricky situation, and I'm not sure who to root for here.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
